skillName: Write positive integers in the form A × 10ⁿ
misconceptionSummary: Students think standard form is just a different way of writing a number — they don't see that A and 10ⁿ carry separate information.

visuals.ruleText: Standard form separates a number into its significant digits (A) and its magnitude (10ⁿ)
visuals.subText: A tells you the precise value; 10ⁿ tells you the order of magnitude

steps.narration: Standard form packs two pieces of information into one expression. The value of A holds all the significant digits. The power of ten tells you roughly how big the number is — its order of magnitude.

lines.text: Ensures exactly one digit before the decimal point

steps.narration: Keeping A between one and ten ensures there is always exactly one non-zero digit before the decimal point. This makes it trivial to compare sizes: the number with the larger exponent is always larger.
Comparing three point two times ten to the six with eight point nine times ten to the four: the exponents six and four immediately reveal which is larger — six wins, regardless of the A values.
Three point two times ten to the six. A is three point two — two significant figures. The exponent six says the number is in the millions.

misconceptionStrip.text: Common mistake: writing 32 × 10⁵ (A not between 1 and 10). Standard form requires exactly one digit before the decimal point in A — rewrite as 3.2 × 10⁶.
misconceptionStrip.audioNarration: A must be at least 1 and less than 10. If A has more than one digit before the decimal, adjust it and change the exponent to compensate.
